theorems.note: Each individually flipped sensitive bit is a sensitive block of size 1.
Block sensitivity is a special case of fractional block sensitivity (set all block weights to 0 or 1).
Shown by Tal [Tal13].
C(f) = max(C_0(f), C_1(f)) by definition.
C(f) = max(C_0(f), C_1(f)) by definition.
A depth-D deterministic decision tree certifies every input with at most D queried bits.
N(f) = C_1(f) by definition (they are the same measure). See equality below.
coN(f) = C_0(f) by definition. See equality below.
Any exact representing polynomial is also a 1/3-approximating polynomial.
The polynomial method lower bound [BBC+01]: any bounded-error quantum algorithm using T queries computes a polynomial of degree ≤ T approximating f.
A deterministic decision tree of depth D computes f via a multilinear polynomial of degree ≤ D (sum over root-to-leaf paths).
Any bounded-error classical randomized algorithm can be simulated by a quantum algorithm with at most the same query cost.
Any one-sided error algorithm (always correct on NO) also has bounded two-sided error, so R_1 is at least as costly.
Any zero-error (Las Vegas) algorithm never errs on YES inputs, making it also one-sided. So R_1 is never harder than R_0.
A deterministic algorithm is a special case of a Las Vegas algorithm.
Any exact quantum algorithm has bounded error (trivially), so Q_E is at least as costly.
A deterministic algorithm can be viewed as an exact quantum algorithm.
Any bounded-error quantum algorithm also computes f with probability > 1/2, so it qualifies as an unbounded-error algorithm.
The general adversary bound is a lower bound for bounded-error quantum query complexity [HLS07].
The GF(2) degree is at most the real polynomial degree, since any real multilinear polynomial can be reduced mod 2.
A deterministic decision tree gives a representation over GF(2) of degree at most its depth.
Any co-one-sided algorithm (always correct on YES) also has bounded two-sided error.
A zero-error algorithm never errs on NO inputs, making it also co-one-sided.

theorems.description: Q(f) = Theta(Adv±(f)) for all total Boolean f
theorems.note: Reichardt's tight adversary theorem [Rei11]: bounded-error quantum query complexity is characterised exactly (up to constant factors) by the general adversary bound.
Definitional equivalence: nondeterministic query complexity equals 1-certificate complexity.
Definitional equivalence: co-nondeterministic query complexity equals 0-certificate complexity.
Shown by Tal [Tal13]: the square of the approximate degree is polynomially equivalent to the fractional block sensitivity.
From D(f) ≤ C_0(f) · C_1(f) ≤ C(f)^2 [Mon14].
Saks and Wigderson [SW86] proved R_0(f) ≥ Omega(sqrt(D(f))), implying D(f) = O(R_0(f)^2). Achieved with equality by the AND-OR tree.
The resolution of the Sensitivity Conjecture by Huang [Hua19]. Combined with s ≤ bs, all combinatorial measures are now known to be polynomially equivalent.
Nisan and Szegedy [NS94] proved deg(f) ≥ sqrt(bs(f)), equivalently bs(f) ≤ deg(f)^2.
Shown by Sherstov [She13] and related work: adeg(f) ≥ sqrt(bs(f)), implying bs ≤ adeg^2.
Follows from combining: Q ≥ adeg, adeg^2 ≥ bs (Sherstov), bs ≥ s (trivial), s^2 ≥ bs (Huang), and D ≤ C^2 ≤ bs^2·s^2 ≤ s^6 ≤ bs^3 ≤ adeg^6 ≤ Q^6. See [ABKRT21] for a tight chain.
Follows from D ≤ C^2 and C ≤ O(R^{3/2}) [ABK16], giving D = O(R^3). The tight relationship between D and R remains open.

theorems.description: D(f) ≤ N(f) · coN(f) = C_1(f) · C_0(f) for all f
theorems.note: A classical result [Nis91]: the product of nondeterministic and co-nondeterministic complexities upper bounds deterministic complexity.

theorems.description: R_0(f) ≤ sqrt(D(f) · C(f)) for all f
theorems.note: A refined bound relating the Las Vegas query complexity to the geometric mean of D and C.

theorems.description: deg(f) ≤ 2 · Q_E(f) for all f, i.e., Q_E(f) ≥ ceil(deg(f)/2)
theorems.note: From the polynomial method [BBC+01]: any T-query exact quantum algorithm computes a polynomial of degree ≤ 2T.

oracle_separations.description: Q(f) vs R(f): The OR function gives Q(OR_n) = Theta(sqrt(n)) while R(OR_n) = Theta(n), a quadratic separation. This is tight by the BBBV lower bound.
R(f) vs D(f): AND-OR tree gives R(f) = Theta(n^{0.793}) while D(f) = n (conjectured tight gap of n^{0.793}).
Q(f) vs D(f): Simon's algorithm and related problems give exponential separations between Q and D in the oracle model.
UPP(f) vs Q(f): The Forrelation function achieves UPP(f) = 1 while Q(f) = Omega(sqrt(n)), an asymptotically maximal separation [AA15].
adeg(f) vs deg(f): The Majority function has adeg(MAJ_n) = Theta(sqrt(n)) but deg(MAJ_n) = n.
R1(f) vs R(f): The OR function has R_1(OR_n) = Theta(n) while R(OR_n) = Theta(sqrt(n)), a quadratic separation.
s(f) vs bs(f): Rubinstein [Rub95] exhibited a function with bs(f) = Omega(s(f)^2), showing Huang's bound is tight.
